questions.prompt: Complete: "I was having a shower ___ the phone rang."
questions.explanation: when introduces the short, completed action (the phone rang) that interrupts the long one: …when the phone rang. "while" goes with the long action in progress, "since" marks a start point, and "during" is followed by a noun, not a clause.

questions.prompt: Complete: "___ we were driving home, we saw an accident."
questions.explanation: while introduces the long action in progress (we were driving): While we were driving home… The short event (we saw an accident) is in the past simple. "For" marks a length of time and "until" marks an end point, neither of which fits here.

questions.prompt: Complete: "While she was studying, he ___ TV."
questions.explanation: When two long actions happen in parallel, both take the past continuous: While she was studying, he was watching TV. "watched" (past simple) would suggest a single short event, "watch/watches" are present forms.

questions.prompt: Complete: "They ___ dinner when the guests arrived."
questions.explanation: The long background action in progress takes the past continuous, and with they we use were: They were having dinner when the guests arrived. "was having" is the wrong agreement, "had" loses the in-progress sense, and "are having" is present.

questions.prompt: Which sentence describes two actions happening at the same time?

options.text: I was sleeping when the storm started.
While Dad was cooking, Mum was setting the table.
She was reading when the door slammed.
We were leaving when it began to snow.

questions.explanation: Two long actions in parallel use the past continuous in both halves: While Dad was cooking, Mum was setting the table. The other three each pair a long action (past continuous) with a single short interruption (past simple), so they are not two simultaneous ongoing actions.

questions.prompt: Complete: "While I ___ for the bus, it started to rain."
questions.explanation: After while, the long action in progress takes the past continuous: While I was waiting for the bus… The short event (it started to rain) is past simple. "waited" is past simple, "wait" is the base, and "am waiting" is present.
